Okay, so now let's jump to the end of the body of your script. Where you ask for the order for the first time. With something like, you know, and believe me, sir, if I'm even half right, the only problem you'll have is I didn't call you six months ago and get you started dead. Sound fair enough? And then they say something like, oh yeah, it sounds pretty good, Jordan, but let me think about it. Now my question to you is this. And they say that first time, let me think about it. Let me call you back. Let me talk to my wife. Where are you on the straight line? So watch, this is the open and this is the close. And here are your boundaries above and below the line. Now my question is this. Where are we on the sale after you've done your qualifying and you've given your initial presentation and asked for the order for the first time? Where are you exactly? I refer to this as the back half of the sale. So who thinks we're here at this point right here? Okay. And who thinks we're over here? Get further down. And who thinks we're actually over here more towards the beginning? Well the last group is right. We're over here towards the beginning. The term back half of the sale is just an expression. It's actually the back 90% of the sale. And that's where all the magic is happening. It's that point where you've already done your qualifying and you've made your opening pitch and asked for the order for the first time and gotten that first, let me think about it. That's where the sale actually begins. See any monkey can read through some qualifying questions and recite a pitch. This is the part of the sale where the rubber meets the road. It's where you finally have the opportunity to earn your paycheck and define yourself as a world-class close, which is exactly what you'll be once you become even reasonably competent with the straight line system. So again, you need to reframe things in your own mind before you even pick up the phone or walk through someone's door that when you get that first objection, it's not a sign that the sale's going south. It's actually a sign you finally have the chance to do your job, which is to close people who are sitting on the fence. That's what a great salesperson does. He doesn't turn nos in T.S.'s. He turns everything else in T.S.'s, meaning all the let me think about it and call you back and got to speak to my wives and business partners and Santhe and the two three, all that stuff. He turns those people in T.S.'s. That's what a top close is all about. So when you say, sound fair enough, and they say, let me think about it, that's where the sale begins right here. It begins right at this spot when they give you that first no.
